talk_title: Asymmetric Emergence of Cardiovascular Disease in Hematologic Malignancy Survivors

speaker.name: Yize Song
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis

models.name: All of Us research program cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: hematologic malignancy survivors vs CVD patients

MOA_summary: Analyzed bi-directional risk between hematologic malignancy survivors and cardiovascular disease using All of Us cohorts, identifying high early CVD incidence especially in MDS and multiple myeloma, and implicating proteasome inhibitor treatment as a potential driver.
key_findings: CVD incidence sharply increases within first 2 years after hematologic malignancy diagnosis, median time 2.4 years.
MDS and multiple myeloma patients have the highest CVD risk in the first five years.
Arrhythmias are the most common CVD subtype post‑diagnosis.
Proteasome inhibitor exposure yields the highest composite cardiotoxicity score among treatments.
In vitro Cell Village assay shows proteasome inhibitor induces inflammatory/dysfunctional cardiac cell states and cell death.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms link proteasome inhibition to specific cardiac cell dysfunction?
Can identified protective markers be targeted to mitigate cardiotoxicity?

talk_title: Decoding Melanoma Immunogenicity: A Comprehensive Proteogenomic, Immunopeptidomic, and Metabolomic Atlas

biomarkers.name: CD8 T cell fraction
biomarkers.finding: Positive correlation with tumor antigen count per sample (p<0.001)

biomarkers.name: HLA‑1 peptide count
biomarkers.finding: Higher counts associate with better survival in ICI‑treated patients

MOA_summary: Integrated 13‑layer multi‑omics atlas of melanoma revealed that high HLA‑I peptide presentation and tumor antigens from MAGEA genes drive CD8 T‑cell infiltration and improved ICI survival, while distinct ubiquitination and glycosylation patterns define prognostic subgroups.
key_findings: Identified 1,138 unique tumor antigen candidates, >50% absent from IEDB.
Tumor antigen count positively correlates with CD8 T‑cell infiltration and ICI‑treated survival.
MAGEA3/4/12 antigen events strongly associate with increased CD8 T‑cell fraction.
A ubiquitin‑high interferon subtype shows worse survival; RNF4‑DHX9 and BIRC7‑AKT1 ubiquitin pairs are prognostic.
A lysosome‑high glycoproteomic subtype displays sex‑specific survival: poor in males, neutral/beneficial in females, linked to GPUMB fucosylation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Sex‑specific impact of GPUMB fucosylation suggests differential resistance mechanisms.
open_questions: Can identified tumor antigens be leveraged for personalized vaccines?
What drives the sex‑specific lysosome phenotype?

talk_title: Endocrine Therapy Adherence and 10‑Year Risk of Cardiometabolic Outcomes Among Women with Hormone Receptor‑Positive Breast Cancer

biomarkers.name: adherence (PDC ≥80%)
biomarkers.finding: Adherent patients had higher 10‑yr hypertension risk (29% vs 20%)

MOA_summary: Using a prospective Kaiser cohort, proportion of days covered measured endocrine therapy adherence and sequentially doubly robust estimation linked longer AI use to higher 10‑year hypertension, dyslipidemia, and diabetes risk, while both AI and tamoxifen adherence reduced mortality.
key_findings: Five‑year endocrine therapy adherence increased 10‑yr hypertension risk by 9 percentage points.
AI use, but not tamoxifen, was associated with higher dyslipidemia (33% vs 26%) and diabetes (13% vs 9.5%) risks.
Both AI and tamoxifen adherence reduced all‑cause mortality (18% vs 28%) and breast cancer‑specific mortality (6‑8% vs 13%).
Adherent patients were younger, higher socioeconomic status, and had fewer baseline comorbidities.
open_questions: How does differential healthcare utilization affect observed cardiometabolic risks?
Would extending AI therapy beyond five years further increase cardiometabolic events?

talk_title: Comprehensive Phenotypic Mapping of ALT Regulation Expands the Landscape of Targetable Suppressors

speaker.name: Bill DiPlas
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Department of Radiation Oncology

MOA_summary: Developed Alt‑Flow‑FISH to quantify ALT activity, performed genome‑wide CRISPR screens identifying proteasome components and akirin‑2 as ALT suppressors; proteasome loss or inhibition accumulates PML, drives hyper‑ALT and cell death, suggesting proteasome inhibition as a therapeutic strategy for ALT cancers.
key_findings: Alt‑Flow‑FISH enables high‑throughput single‑cell ALT activity measurement.
Genome‑wide CRISPR screen uncovered numerous ALT suppressors, notably proteasome subunits (PSMB1, PSMB5, PSMB6) and akirin‑2.
Proteasome depletion leads to PML protein accumulation and hyper‑ALT phenotype with increased c‑circles.
Overexpression of PML alone induces hyper‑ALT and cell death; bortezomib recapitulates this effect.
Pathway analysis highlighted DNA repair, replication, proteasome, and Fanconi anemia pathways as ALT regulators.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Proteasome inhibition may affect normal cells; therapeutic window needs definition.

combination_strategies.rationale: ATR inhibition also tips ALT balance toward hyper‑ALT lethality
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in ALT‑positive models

open_questions: Is ALT hyper‑activation uniformly lethal across diverse ALT tumor types?
Can proteasome inhibition be combined safely with DNA‑damage agents?

talk_title: Protein Complex Remodeling in Metastatic Breast Cancer Cells

speaker.name: Xiaoyu Zhao
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Troy Atticher Lab

MOA_summary: Integrated confocal imaging, IP‑MS, SEC‑MS, and single‑cell proteomics with self‑supervised deep learning to map dynamic protein complex reorganization in TNBC cells under chemotherapy, revealing treatment‑specific assembly/disassembly of complexes such as stress response, transcriptional activation, and SWI/SNF loss.
key_findings: HDAC8 relocalizes from cytosol/nucleus to nucleus after paclitaxel.
HDAC1 interaction partners shift with treatment, indicating dynamic complex remodeling.
Identified >100 protein complexes; ~50% are novel assemblies.
Stress response complex emerges uniquely in vernostat‑treated cells.
Transcriptional activation complex CBP‑300 appears only after paclitaxel.
SWI/SNF complex is lost under both chemotherapies.
open_questions: Do identified dynamic complexes predict patient response or resistance?
Can targeting emergent complexes improve chemosensitivity?

talk_title: ASP2998 – TROP2‑Targeted Immunomodulatory ADC with Dual Payloads Demonstrates Potent Efficacy and Favorable Safety

speaker.name: Mark Orr
speaker.affiliation: Astellas Pharma Global Development, Seattle, WA (collaboration with Sutro)

models.name: MC38‑hTROP2 mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: large tumors (~500 mm³) treated with ASP2998

clinical_data.metric: Tumor regression in large MC38‑hTROP2 model
clinical_data.value: Complete regression at high dose, tumor stasis at lower dose
100% rejection of secondary MC38‑hTROP2 challenge after ASP2998 cure

MOA_summary: ASP2998 is a humanized IgG1 TROP2‑directed ADC delivering a topoisomerase‑I inhibitor and a STING agonist; tumor cell killing releases the STING agonist, activating innate immunity and T‑cell responses, resulting in tumor regression and durable immune memory.
key_findings: Dual‑payload ADC shows enhanced potency in PBMC co‑culture versus cytotoxic‑only ADCs.
In vivo MC38‑hTROP2 model: ASP2998 induces tumor regression and complete protection upon rechallenge.
PK data reveal >99.9% of drug remains intact; free payloads are ~1,000‑fold lower.
GLP repeat‑dose study shows manageable safety profile; target organs at high dose include cornea, kidney, urethra, trachea.
Low IL‑6 induction suggests limited cytokine release syndrome risk.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Efficacy depends on TROP2 expression; threshold not yet defined in trial.

combination_strategies.rationale: STING agonist may convert cold tumors to hot, synergizing with checkpoint blockade

open_questions: What is the optimal TROP2 expression cutoff for patient selection?
How does the STING agonist payload affect systemic immunity and safety?

talk_title: Targeting EYA4 Disrupts Genome Stability in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer

speaker.name: Daniela Garcia
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas San Antonio, Greehey Children’s Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: TNBC cells with high EYA4 expression

MOA_summary: EYA4 binds ssDNA via an N‑terminal basic/AT‑hook region; DNA binding enhances its tyrosine phosphatase activity toward γH2AX and RAD51, promoting homologous recombination. Inhibition or depletion of EYA4 induces HR deficiency, γH2AX accumulation, and tumor growth suppression.
key_findings: EYA4 is hyper‑methylated yet overexpressed in TNBC; highest in MDA‑MB‑231.
EYA4 depletion reduces primary tumor growth and lung metastasis in vivo.
EYA4 binds ssDNA with nanomolar affinity; phosphomimetic mutation reduces binding.
DNA binding markedly stimulates EYA4 tyrosine phosphatase activity.
Small‑molecule inhibitor CID‑XXXX89 reduces HR efficiency (dose‑dependent GFP loss) and cell viability.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential selectivity challenge: phosphatase inhibition may affect normal DNA repair.

combination_strategies.rationale: EYA4 inhibition induces HR deficiency, sensitizing cells to PARP blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction in viability in TNBC models

open_questions: Can EYA4 inhibition overcome PARP resistance in BRCA‑wildtype TNBC?
What biomarkers predict response to EYA4 inhibitors in patient tumors?
